prompt: Before administering a medication, the LPN/LVN should verify:

options.text: Right client, drug, dose, route, time, and documentation (plus allergies/assessment as indicated)
Only the room number without identifiers
That a coworker said it was fine without checking the MAR
Color of the pill alone as the sole check

explanation: Rights of medication administration reduce errors; use two identifiers.

distractorExplanations.b: Room number is not a unique ID.

prompt: A client on fall precautions should have:

options.text: All four side rails up as soft restraints without an order for convenience
Call light in reach, nonskid footwear, bed in low position, and clutter-free path as appropriate
Socks with slick soles encouraged for speed
Call light placed across the room to encourage walking alone at night

explanation: Fall prevention is multifactorial; restraints require orders and least-restrictive approach.

distractorExplanations.a: Four side rails can be restraint.

prompt: When receiving a telephone order, the nurse should:

options.text: Guess incomplete doses without clarifying
Ask a visitor to take the order
Write it down and read back to the provider for confirmation per facility policy
Document later from memory after several hours without read-back

explanation: Read-back prevents verbal order errors.

prompt: Priority action when a fire is discovered in a client room (RACE) begins with:

options.text: Running to post on social media
Collecting personal belongings first for all staff
Ignoring the alarm if it is inconvenient
Rescue/remove clients in immediate danger

explanation: RACE: Rescue, Alarm, Contain, Extinguish/Evacuate as trained.

prompt: Proper use of a gait belt includes:

options.text: Applying snugly around the waist over clothing and holding underhand while ambulating as appropriate
Using it to lift a client vertically alone from the floor after an unwitnessed fall without assessment
Applying over bare skin tightly enough to bruise intentionally
Attaching it to a wheelchair to drag the client

explanation: Gait belts assist balance; post-fall assessment precedes moving.

prompt: Sterile technique is required for:

options.text: Taking an oral temperature with a clean probe cover
Inserting an indwelling urinary catheter (as typically taught)
Feeding a client a meal
Making an unoccupied bed with clean linen

explanation: Invasive procedures that enter sterile body cavities require aseptic/sterile technique.

prompt: A client with a known latex allergy needs:

options.text: Increased latex exposure to desensitize in the hospital without order
No allergy documentation
Latex-free gloves/equipment and allergy communication on the chart/band per policy
Powdered latex gloves preferentially

explanation: Allergy prevention is a safety priority.

prompt: When prioritizing care using ABC/safety, which client does the LPN see first?

options.text: A client requesting a snack refill
A client needing routine linen change
A client asking what time discharge teaching will occur later today
A client with new stridor and difficulty breathing after a procedure

explanation: Airway/breathing threats outrank comfort and routine tasks.

prompt: Incident reports should be:

options.text: Objective, factual, and completed per policy without blaming language in the medical record narrative improperly
Copied into the chart as a punitive essay naming coworkers with speculation
Hidden if no one noticed
Used to threaten clients

explanation: Incident reports improve systems; follow facility documentation rules carefully.

distractorExplanations.b: Keep charting factual; follow policy on referencing reports.

prompt: Safe narcotic counts require:

options.text: One person discarding leftovers without witness when policy requires witness
Two authorized nurses verifying discrepancies per policy before end of shift when required
Borrowing meds from another client’s drawer
Documenting waste after leaving the unit

explanation: Controlled substance accountability is a legal safety requirement.

prompt: For a client in contact precautions, the nurse should:

options.text: Skip hand hygiene because gloves were worn
Take the isolation gown to the cafeteria
Wear gown and gloves for contact with the client/environment as indicated and dedicate equipment when possible
Share BP cuffs without cleaning when dedicated gear is required

explanation: Transmission-based precautions prevent MDRO spread; hand hygiene after PPE removal.

prompt: A client refuses a medication. The LPN should:

options.text: Crush and hide it in food without consent
Force the client’s mouth open
Document as given when it was not
Explore concerns, educate, withhold as refused, notify the provider/RN per policy, and document

explanation: Informed refusal is a client right; covert meds without consent are unethical/illegal.

prompt: Which action reduces healthcare-associated infection risk with urinary catheters?

options.text: Maintain a closed drainage system and keep the bag below bladder level
Routine daily irrigation with tap water for all clients
Disconnect tubing frequently to “rinse”
Place the bag on the abdomen during transport for convenience

explanation: CAUTI prevention bundles emphasize closed systems and dependent drainage.

prompt: When using a fire extinguisher (PASS):

options.text: Spray toward people’s faces first
Pull pin, Aim at base, Squeeze, Sweep
Aim at the top of flames only always
Ignore the pin and throw the extinguisher

explanation: PASS is standard extinguisher training.

distractorExplanations.c: Aim at the base of the fire.

prompt: Delegation to UAP is appropriate for:

options.text: Initial teaching of a complex insulin regimen
Assessment of a new onset chest pain
Stable clients’ routine vital signs and hygiene within UAP training
Titration of IV vasoactive drips

explanation: LPNs/RNs retain assessment, teaching, and unstable client judgment tasks per state NPA.

distractorExplanations.b: New chest pain needs licensed assessment.
distractorExplanations.d: IV vasoactive titration is beyond UAP/typically RN.

prompt: Primary prevention aims to:

options.text: Prevent disease before it occurs (e.g., immunizations, safety education)
Only provide hospice care
Treat end-stage complications exclusively
Ignore screening recommendations

explanation: Primary = prevention; secondary = screening; tertiary = limit disability.

distractorExplanations.c: Treatment of advanced disease is tertiary.

prompt: A 65-year-old client should be encouraged regarding vaccines to discuss with the provider:

options.text: Never receiving any vaccines after age 30
Age-appropriate immunizations such as influenza annually and others per CDC adult schedule
Only childhood vaccines repeated yearly without guidance
Avoiding all preventive care visits

explanation: LPNs reinforce vaccine education; schedules update—refer to current CDC guidance/provider.

prompt: Teaching about sunscreen should include:

options.text: Sunburn is required for vitamin D and is harmless
Tanning beds are risk-free
Use broad-spectrum SPF as directed, reapply, and seek shade—especially peak UV hours
Sunscreen makes skin cancer more likely always

explanation: UV protection is skin cancer primary prevention.

distractorExplanations.d: Sunscreen reduces risk when used properly.

prompt: A client with hypertension is learning lifestyle measures. Which teaching point is appropriate?

options.text: Stop all medications if you feel well without contacting the provider
Add unlimited salt substitutes without checking potassium risks if on certain meds
Ignore blood pressure checks at home if advised
Reduce sodium intake, maintain healthy weight, exercise as approved, and take meds as prescribed

explanation: Lifestyle + adherence; don’t stop antihypertensives abruptly without guidance.

distractorExplanations.b: Some salt substitutes are high in K+.

prompt: Prenatal teaching: folic acid is emphasized early to help prevent:

options.text: Neural tube defects
Dental caries in the neonate exclusively
Clubbing of adult fingers
Sensorineural hearing loss from cerumen

explanation: Folic acid before/early pregnancy reduces NTD risk.

distractorExplanations.b: Not the primary folic acid rationale.

prompt: Safe sleep teaching for infants includes:

options.text: Stomach sleeping with pillows in the crib
Place infant supine on a firm surface without soft bedding/loose blankets
Co-sleeping on soft couches as preferred teaching
Bumper pads and stuffed toys filling the crib

explanation: AAP safe sleep: alone, back, crib—reduces SIDS risk.

distractorExplanations.a: Prone with soft items increases risk.

prompt: Secondary prevention example:

options.text: Influenza immunization before flu season
Physical therapy after a completed stroke to maximize function
Mammography screening per guidelines
Wearing a seat belt every time you drive

explanation: Screening detects disease early (secondary). Immunizations and seat belts are primary; rehab after disease is tertiary.

distractorExplanations.b: Rehab after stroke is tertiary prevention.

prompt: A client learning breast self-awareness should be taught to report:

options.text: Only changes that are painful, ignoring painless lumps
Nothing until annual visits even if a hard fixed mass appears
Normal cyclic tenderness as an emergency every month without pattern context
New lumps, nipple discharge, skin changes, or persistent breast changes

explanation: Painless lumps still need evaluation; know personal baselines.

distractorExplanations.c: Cyclic changes can be normal—teach patterns vs red flags.

prompt: Smoking cessation counseling by nurses is important because tobacco use:

options.text: Increases risk of cancer, heart disease, stroke, and respiratory disease
Has no health effects
Protects against COPD
Is recommended in pregnancy

explanation: Ask-Advise-Refer style brief interventions are part of health promotion.

prompt: Growth charts in pediatrics are used to:

options.text: Diagnose all genetic diseases alone
Track patterns of weight/height/head circumference over time
Replace immunizations
Determine adult career aptitude

explanation: Trends matter more than single points; report crossing major percentiles per protocol.

prompt: Teaching for a client starting a new exercise program after sedentary lifestyle should include:

options.text: Begin with maximal heavy lifting daily without assessment
Ignore chest pain as normal
Start gradually, get provider clearance if high risk, and stop for chest pain/severe dyspnea
Avoid hydration intentionally

explanation: Progressive activity with warning-sign education is safer.

prompt: Dental health teaching for preschoolers includes:

options.text: Putting the child to bed with a bottle of juice routinely
Never cleaning teeth until age 10
Sharing toothbrushes among siblings
Supervised brushing with a rice-grain/smear of fluoride toothpaste as age-appropriate and limiting sugary drinks

explanation: Early dental habits prevent caries; avoid juice bottles at bedtime.

prompt: Osteoporosis prevention teaching for older adults often includes:

options.text: Weight-bearing exercise as tolerated, calcium/vitamin D per provider, and fall prevention
Complete bedrest to protect bones
Avoiding all dairy/alternatives without plan when deficient
Removing grab bars to encourage independence via risk

explanation: Bone health + fall risk reduction prevents fractures.

prompt: A client asks when routine colorectal cancer screening often begins for average risk adults (general U.S. guidance knowledge):

options.text: Only after age 90
Around age 45 for average-risk adults per updated USPSTF-aligned counseling—confirm with provider
Never if asymptomatic, ever
At age 15 for everyone as colonoscopy mandatory yearly

explanation: Screening ages evolve; nurses teach to follow current guidelines/provider advice (commonly starting mid-40s now).

distractorExplanations.a: Far too late as a general rule.
distractorExplanations.d: Yearly colonoscopy from 15 is not standard average-risk guidance.

prompt: Health literacy–friendly teaching uses:

options.text: Only medical jargon to sound professional
Long uninterrupted lectures without checking understanding
Plain language, teach-back, and limited key points with written materials at appropriate level
Assuming all clients read at college level

explanation: Teach-back confirms understanding and improves adherence.

prompt: A client says, “I feel like life is not worth living.” Priority nursing action is to:

options.text: Ask directly about suicidal ideation and plans and ensure safety/notify the RN-provider per protocol
Change the subject to the weather permanently
Leave the client alone for privacy for several hours without assessment
Promise total confidentiality even if there is an imminent plan

explanation: Direct screening and safety trump avoidance; duty to protect limits confidentiality when harm is imminent.

distractorExplanations.c: Do not isolate without safety plan.

prompt: Therapeutic communication technique—restating—means:

options.text: Giving false reassurance “everything will be fine”
Repeating the main idea to show listening and encourage clarification
Asking “why would you do that?” judgmentally
Talking about your own divorce details extensively

explanation: Restating/reflecting facilitate expression; avoid “why” and false reassurance.

prompt: A client with alcohol use disorder is hospitalized for detox. Priority concern early is:

options.text: Scheduling career counseling as the first hour priority over medical monitoring
Allowing unlimited visitors bringing alcohol
Safety monitoring for withdrawal complications (e.g., seizures, DT risk) per protocol
Ignoring CIWA/assessment tools ordered

explanation: Physiologic withdrawal can be life-threatening; medical stabilization first.

prompt: Defense mechanism: projection is:

options.text: Returning to earlier developmental behaviors
Consciously deciding to postpone attention to a stressor temporarily (suppression)
Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activity (sublimation)
Attributing one’s own unacceptable feelings to others

explanation: Know common defense mechanisms for psychosocial integrity items.

distractorExplanations.a: Regression is returning to earlier behaviors.

prompt: For a client with dementia who is agitated at sundown, helpful approaches include:

options.text: Calm environment, consistent caregivers, toileting/pain check, and simple reassurance
Arguing about the exact year repeatedly for long periods
Overstimulating with multiple loud activities at once
Using restraints as first-line without assessment

explanation: Address unmet needs; least restrictive measures first.

prompt: Grief that is delayed or inhibited may appear as:

options.text: Only joyful celebration without any loss context ever
Minimal emotional expression initially with later unexpected distress
A vital sign value
A type of microorganism

explanation: Recognize complicated grief patterns and support/refer.

distractorExplanations.a: Grief varies culturally—but delayed grief is a recognized pattern.

prompt: A client with schizophrenia says, “The TV is telling me to hurt myself.” The nurse should:

options.text: Agree that the TV is a reliable commander
Leave without assessing intent
Take the command hallucination seriously, assess safety, and report promptly for evaluation
Whisper about the client in the hallway within earshot

explanation: Command hallucinations require urgent safety assessment.

prompt: Anxiety reduction before surgery can be aided by:

options.text: Withholding all information to prevent worry
Making jokes about death in the OR
Skipping consent discussion
Providing clear procedural information and allowing questions

explanation: Information reduces fear of the unknown; informed consent remains essential.

prompt: Domestic violence screening should be conducted:

options.text: Privately, with sensitivity, and with knowledge of resources/reporting laws
Only with the partner present translating
As accusations in front of visitors
Never in healthcare settings

explanation: Screen alone; follow mandatory reporting rules for vulnerable populations/state laws.

prompt: A client with bulimia nervosa after purging needs monitoring for:

options.text: Only high hemoglobin as the sole issue
Electrolyte imbalances and dental/esophageal injury risks
Improved enamel strength
Hyperkalemia exclusively always

explanation: Purging risks hypokalemia, alkalosis, enamel erosion—safety priority.

distractorExplanations.a: Electrolytes more critical than Hgb alone.

prompt: Cultural competence includes:

options.text: Assuming all members of a group share identical beliefs
Refusing interpreters to save time
Asking about health beliefs/preferences rather than stereotyping
Mocking ritual practices

explanation: Assess individuals; use qualified interpreters.

prompt: Milieu therapy refers to:

options.text: Only intramuscular medications
Surgical debridement
Dietary tray color coding alone
Using the therapeutic environment and structured interactions to support recovery

explanation: Psychiatric nursing uses milieu as a treatment tool.

distractorExplanations.a: Meds may be part of care but aren’t the definition.

prompt: When a client is angry and pacing, an appropriate initial approach is:

options.text: Maintain safe distance, calm tone, offer to listen, and ensure exit access for staff
Cornering the client in a small room alone
Matching anger with yelling
Touching suddenly from behind

explanation: De-escalation prioritizes safety and respect.

prompt: A client facing limb amputation expresses body image disturbance. The nurse should:

options.text: Say “at least you’re alive” as the only response and leave
Encourage expression of feelings and involve rehab/support resources
Forbid looking at the site forever
Ignore the topic as unimportant

explanation: Body image work is legitimate psychosocial care.

distractorExplanations.c: Gradual viewing may be therapeutic with support.

prompt: Confidentiality in mental health settings means:

options.text: Sharing details in the elevator for teaching without need
Posting client stories online anonymized poorly with identifiers
Protect PHI while explaining limits (harm to self/others, abuse reporting)
Promising never to report child abuse disclosures

explanation: Tarasoff-type duties and mandated reporting create limits to confidentiality.

prompt: A client with heart failure has 2+ pitting edema and crackles. Priority nursing action is often to:

options.text: Elevate HOB, monitor SpO2/respiratory status, and report changes; administer ordered diuretics/O2 per plan
Encourage large free-water boluses immediately
Place flat supine for comfort only without assessment
Stop all ordered weights and I&O

explanation: Fluid overload threatens gas exchange; follow ABC and orders.

prompt: Hypoglycemia in a conscious client who can swallow is treated with:

options.text: IV insulin bolus by the LPN without order
15 g rapid-acting carbohydrate, recheck per protocol (15-15 rule variants)
Ignoring diaphoresis and confusion
Exercise immediately without treating glucose

explanation: Treat low glucose first; unresponsive clients need emergency glucagon/IV dextrose per protocol/RN-provider.

prompt: A client on warfarin should be taught to:

options.text: Double doses if a meal is missed without guidance
Start high-dose NSAIDs freely for pain without checking interactions
Keep vitamin K intake consistent and report bleeding/bruising; keep INR appointments
Stop warfarin the day before dental cleaning without telling providers

explanation: Anticoagulation teaching focuses on consistency, bleeding signs, and interaction awareness.

prompt: Post-op client with PCA morphine is difficult to arouse with RR 6/min. Priority action:

options.text: Give another opioid dose for sleep
Leave to finish charting first
Encourage family to press the PCA button repeatedly
Stop the PCA/opioids per protocol, support airway/breathing, stimulate, and notify RN/provider; prepare naloxone as ordered

explanation: Opioid-induced respiratory depression is an emergency.

prompt: Signs of wound infection include:

options.text: Increasing redness, warmth, purulent drainage, odor, fever
Approximated edges with scant serous drainage on day 1 only as definitive infection
Normal pruritus of healing without other signs as infection proof
Absent drainage and afebrile state as infection

explanation: Differentiate normal inflammatory healing from infection; report changes.

distractorExplanations.b: Early scant serous can be expected.

prompt: A client with COPD should be taught pursed-lip breathing to:

options.text: Breathe as rapidly as possible at all times
Prolong exhalation and prevent airway collapse/air trapping
Hold breath for 2 minutes repeatedly
Avoid any controlled breathing techniques

explanation: Pursed-lip breathing improves ventilation patterns in COPD.

prompt: Potassium chloride IV should never be:

options.text: Given as a diluted infusion per policy when ordered
Monitored with labs when replacement is underway
Given as an IV push/bolus
Held when hyperkalemia is present per order

explanation: IVP KCl can cause fatal dysrhythmias; infusions use pumps/dilution rules.

prompt: For a client with a cast, report immediately:

options.text: Mild itching under the cast without neurovascular change
Ability to wiggle toes with warm pink color
Normal capillary refill <2–3 seconds
Increasing pain, pallor, paresthesia, pulselessness, paralysis (compartment syndrome signs)

explanation: Compartment syndrome is limb-threatening—escalate ASAP.

prompt: NG tube placement verification before feeding is best confirmed by:

options.text: Facility-required method (often x-ray initially; pH/markings per policy)—never by air auscultation alone as sole method
Instilling a large feeding immediately after blind insertion without verification
Asking the roommate if it looks right
Checking only that tape is present

explanation: Misplacement risks aspiration; follow current verification standards.

prompt: A client with dehydration may show:

options.text: Bounding pulses and wet crackles as the only dehydration signs
Tachycardia, dry mucous membranes, poor skin turgor, concentrated urine
Jugular venous distention as classic dehydration
Polyuria with dilute urine as dehydration hallmark always

explanation: Volume depletion signs guide fluid resuscitation orders and monitoring.

prompt: Insulin lispro (rapid-acting) should generally be given:

options.text: Once weekly only regardless of meals
Without regard to food, then leaving the client NPO for hours intentionally
At mealtime / when the tray is present per timing rules (onset ~15 min)
Mixed freely into IV TPN bags by LPN without protocol

explanation: Match rapid insulin to carbohydrate intake to prevent hypoglycemia.

distractorExplanations.d: IV insulin is RN/protocol territory typically.

prompt: Early hypoxia sign often includes:

options.text: Cyanosis as the earliest reliable sign always
Bradypnea as the first change in all clients
Warm dry skin exclusively
Restlessness and tachycardia

explanation: Late signs include cyanosis; act on early mental status/VS changes.

distractorExplanations.c: Cool/clammy or diaphoresis may occur—not warm dry as classic early hypoxia.

prompt: A client receiving a blood transfusion develops chills, fever, and back pain. The nurse should:

options.text: Stop the transfusion, keep IV line open with NS per protocol, notify provider, and follow transfusion reaction steps
Speed up the transfusion to finish faster
Ignore symptoms for 30 minutes
Give a second unit immediately through the same tubing without evaluation

explanation: Suspect acute hemolytic/febrile reaction—stop blood, maintain access, escalate.

prompt: Left-sided weakness after stroke: nursing care includes:

options.text: Pulling by the weak arm to reposition
Approach from the intact side as appropriate, protect the weak side, and use assistive devices per PT plan
Ignoring aspiration risk at meals
Placing food on the weak side of the mouth intentionally without assessment

explanation: Safety and rehab principles guide hemiparesis care; dysphagia screens matter.

prompt: Digitalis toxicity warning signs to report include:

options.text: Improved appetite only as toxicity
Isolated happiness without physical signs
Anorexia, nausea, visual changes (halos), and bradycardia/dysrhythmias
Hyperactivity with tachycardia exclusively as the classic dig toxicity pattern

explanation: Narrow therapeutic index—monitor apical pulse and labs (K+, dig level) per orders.

distractorExplanations.d: Bradycardia more classic than isolated tachycardia.
